body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial (a permission classifier/gate exists — but only in the TUI)
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: gap:permission-approval

## Scenario

auto-classified-permission is the SAFE-PATH branch of the permission-classification feature: the agent invokes a benign tool, an in-product classifier auto-APPROVES it WITHOUT firing a permission prompt, and the session must trace `ready → working → ready` and never enter `waiting`. The defining, cell-distinguishing signal is the auto-CLASSIFICATION step (a per-call safe-vs-risky decision that selectively prompts) — not merely "a tool ran with no waiting". The risky-path branch (the prompt actually firing → waiting) is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell.

## Agent supports it (agent_supports: partial)

opencode declares permission_hooks:true (replaydata/agents/opencode/capabilities.json) and tool_calls:true. Its interactive TUI does classify and gate tool execution behind a per-tool approve/deny overlay — so the classifier this cell asserts CAN, in the TUI, decide a benign call is safe and run it without prompting. Marked partial (not yes) because that classifier and its safe-vs-risky decision are TUI-only; the headless path exposes no observable classification step at all.

## Why this cell is NOT recordable today (driver gap: permission-approval)

The classification + any prompt lives ENTIRELY in the interactive TUI. The recording rig drives opencode through the headless `opencode run` path, which AUTO-EXECUTES every tool inside opencode's own sandbox with NO interactive permission prompt and NO observable classification or `waiting` state. PROVEN this sweep: the opencode/auto-executed-tool-call recording captured the local gemma model's `write` tool running automatically INSIDE the working span with zero waiting/approval state (ready → working → ready, no waiting). So on the headless driver the auto-classifier's safe-vs-risky decision never surfaces — a headless `opencode run` recording is indistinguishable from the plain auto-executed-tool-call cell and would prove the wrong thing (a generic ungated tool call, not an auto-CLASSIFICATION gate).

drive-opencode-interactive.sh drives opencode through BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses and implements ONLY `send` / `wait_turn` (a no-op — `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends) / `sleep` in its `case "$TYPE"` dispatch. There is NO approve/deny/classify step (and no `keys` step) to surface or answer a permission classification. The recipe is therefore left applicable:false with no `script`, so recipe-lint.sh cannot flag it (no step-TYPE to reject); the missing capability is a live-REPL permission-approval primitive — the same shape as the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt (gap:permission-approval), user-esc-interrupt (gap:interrupt) and model-switch-midsession (gap:model-select) driver-gap cells.

## daemon side

opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go) — the daemon reads session/turn state from the SQLite store, not from hooks. The store-reading parser/watcher have no permission-gate or `waiting` branch; the canonical rule-0 PermissionPending→waiting gate is a claudecode-hook path opencode's store transport does not feed. For THIS safe-path cell the daemon side is the strong half: the ungated ready→working→ready arc with the no-waiting invariant is exactly what the ProcessOwnedStore source already observes (proven by auto-executed-tool-call). daemon_capability:full per the #476 routing decision (the blocker is the driver, not the daemon); the only UNPROVEN piece is whether the daemon could observe the auto-classification step ITSELF — which cannot be tested until a live-REPL driver can elicit the classifier in the TUI.

## What would unblock it

Replace or augment drive-opencode-interactive.sh with a LIVE-REPL driver (analogous to the tmux-based claudecode/pi/aider drivers): launch the opencode TUI in a tmux pane and `send` a benign tool-inducing prompt so the auto-classifier runs in-product, capturing the safe-path auto-approval (and, for the sibling cell, an approve/deny step to resolve a risky prompt). Only then can the auto-CLASSIFICATION signal be recorded and the daemon's observability of it confirmed.

## Confidence

0.85: the driver grammar (`send|wait_turn|sleep`, blocking `opencode run` per send, no approve/deny/classify step) is read directly from the live driver source; the headless auto-execute-no-prompt-no-classification behavior is proven by the sibling auto-executed-tool-call recording; and the absence of any permission/classification/waiting branch in the store-reading parser/watcher is read directly from source. Not higher only because the unblocked recording (the in-TUI auto-classifier running) has not been captured — that is the developer task this gap defers.
caveats: Driver-gap, not an agent limitation: opencode declares permission_hooks:true and its TUI classifies and gates tools. The blocker is the headless driver — `opencode run` auto-executes tools with no prompt and no observable classification (proven by auto-executed-tool-call), and the driver has no approve/deny/classify step.

recipe-lint.sh does NOT catch this gap: the recipe is left applicable:false with no script, so there is no step-TYPE to reject; the missing capability is a live-REPL permission-approval primitive (same shape as the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt gap:permission-approval cell).

A headless `opencode run` recording of this cell would be indistinguishable from auto-executed-tool-call (a generic ungated tool call) and would prove the wrong thing — the auto-CLASSIFICATION step, the cell's defining signal, only arises in the TUI.

daemon_capability:full is the routing label (the driver is the blocker) and the ungated ready→working→ready arc is proven observable; whether the daemon could observe the classification step itself is UNPROVEN until a live-REPL driver elicits one.

sources.note: case "$TYPE" handles only send|wait_turn|sleep; each `send` launches a BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocess that auto-executes tools with no permission prompt and no observable classification — no approve/deny/classify step to surface or answer the auto-classifier
permission_hooks:true and tool_calls:true — opencode classifies and gates tools, but that classifier lives in the interactive TUI, not the headless `opencode run` path the rig drives

PROOF the headless driver auto-executes tools with no prompt and no classification: the auto-executed-tool-call cell captured gemma's `write` tool running inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state (ready→working→ready, no waiting). A headless auto-classified-permission recording would be indistinguishable from it.
sibling risky-path branch, same gap:permission-approval verdict and same unblock (a live-REPL driver) — these two cells are the safe/risky halves of opencode's TUI-only permission classifier
store-reading parser has NO permission/ask/classify/waiting branch — the canonical rule-0 PermissionPending→waiting gate is a claudecode-hook path opencode's ProcessOwnedStore transport does not feed

the recordable claudecode sibling: safe-path ready→working→ready with the auto-mode classifier approving a benign Bash echo without firing the PermissionRequest hook — the target arc opencode cannot demonstrate headlessly because its classifier is TUI-only
